title: Whiteface (performance)
diff_added: [[Blackface]] is widely considered racist due to its traceable racial links to [[slavery]] and [[racial segregation]].<ref name="Vox">{{cite web|url=https://www.vox.com/2014/10/29/7089591/why-is-blackface-offensive-halloween-costume|title=Don't get what's wrong with blackface? Here's why it's so offensive.|last=Desmond-Harris|first=Jenée|date=October 29, 2014|work=[[Vox (website)|Vox]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190523182549/https://www.vox.com/2014/10/29/7089591/why-is-blackface-offensive-halloween-costume|archive-date=May 23, 2019|url-status=live|access-date=January 10, 2019}}</ref> For this reason, blackface is heavily condemned in modern art forms, while whiteface is occasionally employed in modern times, usually in a comedic context. Those who defend it as art differentiate it from blackface, often arguing that whiteface does not draw on a legacy of racism in the way that blackface does, hence arguing that the intended satire of white lifestyles is not racist.<ref>{{cite web|last=Hannaham|first=James|title=Beyond the Pale|website=New York Magazine|date=28 June 2004|url=https://nymag.com/nymetro/arts/features/9325}}</ref> However, even if whiteface is framed as satire and lacks the same historical weight, it can still be considered prejudicial, as it relies on stereotyping or reducing a group to exaggerated traits for effect.

title: Paul Kohler (politician)
diff_added:
| honorific_suffix    = {{post-nominals|country=GBR|size=100%|MP}}
Kohler was the first person in his extended family to go to university. However, he dropped out from his maths degree, because of touring commitments in the Gotham City Swing Band, a [[New wave music|new wave]] outfit whose greatest claim to fame was playing the last night of the punk venue, [[The Roxy (Covent Garden)|The Roxy]].{{citation needed|date=November 2025}} He subsequently returned to university and graduated in 1986 from [[Jesus College, Cambridge]], with a [[Bachelor of Arts]] (BA) degree in law. During his time at the [[University of Cambridge]], he was president of the college [[May Ball in Cambridge|May Ball]] committee, where he appointed fellow Jesus undergraduate [[Prince Edward, Duke of Edinburgh|Prince Edward]] as a member of his team.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Miller |first1=Compton |title=Pop star to Sue over Royal Ball |url=https://www.britishnewspaperarchive.co.uk/viewer/bl/0004848/19850620/170/0017 |access-date=6 November 2025 |work=Daily Express |date=20 June 1985}}</ref> He was also elected [[Junior Common Room]] president.<ref>{{Cite journal|url=https://www.yourlocalguardian.co.uk/news/11418807.brave-academic-paul-kohler-chased-violent-burglars-after-brutal-wimbledon-assault/|title=Brave academic Paul Kohler chased violent burglars after brutal Wimbledon assault|journal=Your Local Guardian|first=Becky|last=Middleton|date=19 August 2014|accessdate=9 July 2024}}</ref>
Kohler is married to Samantha MacArthur, a company director and Merton Liberal Democrat councillor, with  whom he has four daughters.<ref>{{cite web | last=Clarke | first=Amy | title=Stranger saves daughter of Wimbledon councillor during attempted mugging | website=Wimbledon Times | date=11 January 2023 | url=https://www.wimbledonguardian.co.uk/news/23243693.councillor-paul-kohler-daughter-victim-attempted-wimbledon-mugging/ | access-date=7 July 2024}}</ref>
In 2014 he sustained severe injuries when he was beaten by burglars during a raid at his home. He suffered a fractured [[eye socket]], broken nose and severe internal bleeding.<ref>{{cite news |title=Burglars admit attacking lecturer Paul Kohler in London home |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-london-30213462 |access-date=5 November 2025 |work=BBC News |date=26 November 2014}}</ref><ref name=bbc2018>{{cite web | title=Attack victim Paul Kohler loses police stations battle | website=BBC News | date=20 July 2018 | url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-london-44899075 | access-date=7 July 2024}}</ref>  However, he credits that attack, and the subsequent [[restorative justice]] session in which he met one of his attackers, as the reason for his belated decision to enter active politics.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.standard.co.uk/news/politics/paul-kohler-prime-minister-commons-wimbledon-government-b1207870.html|title=Meeting attacker through restorative justice scheme 'transformed' my life: MP|first=Rhiannon|last=James|date=29 J

title: Neringa Dangvydė
diff_added:
'''Neringa Dangvydė''' was the pen name of '''Neringa Macatė''' (previously Mikalauskienė) (6 April 1975 – 21 March 2020), a Lithuanian writer, poet, book illustrator, and literary critic. She is best known as the author of two children's books. Her debut book, ''Amber Heart'', was published in 2013 and caused public controversy because it depicted [[same-sex relationship]]s in a positive light. Dangvydė sued Lithuania for imposing limits on the distribution of the book at the [[European Convention on Human Rights]]. In January 2023, the case was ruled in her favor.<ref>{{Cite web |title=HUDOC – European Court of Human Rights |url=https://hudoc.echr.coe.int/eng?i=001-222072 |access-date=2023-02-02 |website=hudoc.echr.coe.int}}</ref>
Neringa Dangvydė was born in [[Vilnius]], but spent her childhood and youth in [[Alytus]], where she graduated from the 9th secondary school and an art school. She went to study philosophy at [[Vilnius University]] but quickly switched to literary studies.<ref name=lrytas/> She was a member of the [[Lithuanian Writers' Union]] (since 2017) and of IBBY Lithuania (International Bilingual Literature and Young People's Literature Association).<ref name=lrytas/> She died of cancer on 21 March 2020.<ref name=horba/>
Dangvydė published two books for children: the collection of fairy tales ''Amber Heart'' (''Gintarinė širdis'', 2013) and the novel ''Child with a Star on the Forehead'' (''Vaikas su žvaigžde kaktoje'', 2016).<ref name=lrytas/> The novel explored loneliness and ridicule faced by children from "incomplete" families.<ref name=horba/> While the first book caused public controversy, the second book was awarded the Vytautas Tamulaitis Award<ref name=lrytas/>, which was established in 1998 by the [[Šakiai District Municipality]].<ref name=ibby18/> She also wrote poetry; the collection ''Kiaukuto nešėja'' was published posthumously in 2021. Monika Bertašiūtė-Čiužienė writing for [[15min.lt]] selected the collection as one of the top 11 Lithuanian poetry books of 2021.<ref name=berta/> The collection explores lesbian romance.<ref name=berta/> Just before her death, she finished a novella for [[Young adult fiction|young adults]] ''Ripper'' (''Plėšytojas''), which has not been published.<ref name=rasyt/>
Dangvydė wrote [[literary criticism]] and published in journals active in the cultural field: ''[[Literatūra ir menas]]'', ''Gimtasis žodis'', ''Nemunas'', ''Šiaurės Atėnai'', ''Metai'', ''The Vilnius Review'', and ''Ribinaitis''.<ref name=ibby20/> She also illustrated books, including her own children's books and ''Gugis – girių kaukas ir žmonių draugas'' by {{ill|Justinas Žilinskas|lt}} in 2006.<ref name=ibby20/>
The debut book ''Amber Heart'' (in Lithuanian: ''Gintarinė širdis'') stirred public controversy and scandal: the book was accused of promoting "[[Gay agenda|propaganda of homosexuality]]".<ref name=horba/> The book was an attempt to rewrite classic children's literature, drawing inspiration from the [[Brothers

She went to study philosophy at the [[Vilnius University]] but quickly switched to literary studies.<ref name=lrytas/> She was a member of the [[Lithuanian Writers' Union]] (since 2017), as well as of the IBBY Lithuania (International Bilingual Literature and Young People's Literature Association).<ref name=lrytas/> She died of cancer on 21 March 2020.<ref name=horba/>
Dangvydė published two books for children: the collection of fairy tales ''Amber Heart'' (''Gintarinė širdis'', 2013) and the novel ''Child with a Star on the Forehead'' (''Vaikas su žvaigžde kaktoje'', 2016).<ref name=lrytas/> The novel explored loneliness and ridicule faced by children from "incomplete" families.<ref name=horba/> While the first book caused public controversy, the second book was awarded the Vytautas Tamulaitis Award<ref name=lrytas/> which was established in 1998 by the [[Šakiai District Municipality]].<ref name=ibby18/> She also wrote poetry; the collection ''Kiaukuto nešėja'' was published posthumously in 2021. Monika Bertašiūtė-Čiužienė writing for [[15min.lt]] selected the collection as top 11 of Lithuanian poetry books of 2021.<ref name=berta/> The collection explores lesbian romance.<ref name=berta/> Just before her death, she finished a novella for [[Young adult fiction|young adults]] ''Ripper'' (''Plėšytojas'') which has not been published.<ref name=rasyt/>
Dangvydė wrote [[literary criticism]] and published in journals active in the cultural field: ''[[Literatūra ir menas]]'', ''Gimtasis žodis'', ''Nemunas'', ''Šiaurės Atėnai'', ''Metai'', ''The Vilnius Review'', ''Ribinaitis''.<ref name=ibby20/> She also illustrated books, including her own children's book and ''Gugis – girių kaukas ir žmonių draugas'' by {{ill|Justinas Žilinskas|lt}} in 2006.<ref name=ibby20/>
The debut book ''Amber Heart'' (in Lithuanian: ''Gintarinė širdis'') stirred public controversy and scandal: the book was accused of promoting  "[[Gay agenda|propaganda of homosexuality]]".<ref name=horba/> The book was an attempt to rewrite classic children's literature, drawing inspiration from the [[Brothers Gr

Domovoi Butler serves as [[#Artemis Fowl II|Artemis Fowl II]]'s dedicated bodyguard,<ref name="uncle">{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=1|pages=yes|startpage=28|endpage=29|month=June|year=2008}}</ref><ref name="employer">{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=1|pages=yes|startpage=16|month=June|year=2008}}</ref> continuing a long-standing tradition within the Butler family lineage dating back to the [[Norman conquest]].<ref>{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=1|pages=yes|startpage=15|month=June|year=2008}}</ref> With a formidable physique standing nearly seven feet tall and weighing 200 kilograms, he possesses deep blue eyes characteristic of his lineage.<ref name="description">{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=1|pages=yes|startpage=8|month=June|year=2008}}</ref>
Trained extensively at ''Madame Ko's Bodyguard Academy'', Butler is proficient in various disciplines, including [[Le Cordon Bleu|Cordon Bleu]] cooking, [[marksmanship]], martial arts, emergency medicine, and [[information technology]].<ref>{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=1|pages=yes|startpage=15|endpage=16|month=June|year=2005}}</ref> Renowned as the third deadliest individual globally, his skills surpass those of most adversaries.
In [[Artemis Fowl (book)|''Artemis Fowl'']], Butler achieves a historic feat by disarming an elite squadron of the ''[[LEPretrieval]]'' fairy police force<ref name="LEP">{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=1|pages=yes|startpage=126|endpage=141|month=June|year=2008}}</ref> and defeating a troll<ref name="troll">{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=1|pages=yes|startpage=208|endpage=239|month=June|year=2008}}</ref> using antique armor, alongside his sister Juliet. This incident, broadcast on fairy television, contributes to the LEP's combat curriculum. Throughout the series, Butler's expertise and experience make him a trusted advisor to Artemis, particularly in matters involving the fairy world.
Outside his protective duties, Butler finds solace in reading fiction, introduced to him by Minerva Paradizo. His literary interests encompass classics such as "[[Moby-Dick|Moby Dick]]," "[[Gormenghast (novel)|Gormenghast]]," "[[The Art of War]]," and "[[Gone with the Wind (novel)|Gone with the Wind]]."<ref>{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=5|pages=yes|startpage=381|month=September|year=2006}}</ref> on the book shelf of Butler's residence in the fictional village of Duncade, [[Ireland]],<ref>{{AF Cite Book|booknumber=5|pages=yes|startpage=380|month=September|year=2006}}</ref> where he was awaiting Artemis's return.

'''Dirk Jan Klaas Huntelaar''' (born 12 August 1983), known professionally as '''Klaas-Jan Huntelaar''' ({{IPA|nl|ˌklaːˈɕjɑn  ˈɦʏntəlaːr|-|Klaas-Jan Huntelaar pronunciation.ogg}}),{{efn|In isolation, ''Klaas'' and ''Jan'' are pronounced {{IPA|nl|klaːs|}} and {{IPA|nl|jɑn|}}, respectively.}} is a Dutch former professional [[association football|footballer]] who played as a [[striker (association football)|striker]].
Huntelaar played for [[PSV Eindhoven|PSV]], [[De Graafschap]], [[AGOVV Apeldoorn]], [[SC Heerenveen|Heerenveen]], [[AFC Ajax|Ajax]], [[Real Madrid CF|Real Madrid]] and [[A.C. Milan|AC Milan]],<ref>{{cite web | title= Real Madrid C.F. and AFC Ajax reach agreement for transfer of player | publisher= Realmadrid.com | url= http://www.realmadrid.com/cs/Satellite/en/1202756744881/noticia/ComunicadoOficial/Huntelaar_to_sign_for_Real_Madrid.htm | date= 2 December 2008 | access-date= 1 January 2009 | url-status= dead | archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20090114053624/http://www.realmadrid.com/cs/Satellite/en/1202756744881/noticia/ComunicadoOficial/Huntelaar_to_sign_for_Real_Madrid.htm | archive-date= 14 January 2009 | df= dmy-all }}</ref> before joining [[FC Schalke 04|Schalke 04]] in August 2010, for whom he was the [[List of Bundesliga top scorers|top goalscorer]] in the [[2011–12 Bundesliga]] with 29 league goals.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Bundesliga 2011/2012 - Top Scorer |url=https://www.worldfootball.net/competition/co12/se7094/statistics-goals/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170529170345/http://www.worldfootball.net/goalgetter/bundesliga-2011-2012/ |archive-date=29 May 2017 |access-date=15 June 2025 |website=worldfootball.net |url-status=live |language=en-GB}}</ref> Huntelaar is also [[List of FC Schalke 04 records and statistics#Top goalscorers|Schalke's second highest goalscorer of all time]], behind [[Klaus Fischer]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Rekordlisten - Schalke04.de |url=http://tradition.schalke04.de/de/portraet/erfolge/rekordlisten/page/61--61--.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150922055249/http://tradition.schalke04.de/de/portraet/erfolge/rekordlisten/page/61--61--.html |archive-date=22 September 2015 |access-date=15 June 2025 |website=tradition.schalke04.de |language=de}}</ref>
By the start of 2003, it was clear that Huntelaar's path into the first team was blocked,<ref>{{cite web | title=PSV looking to loan out kids | publisher=Sky Sports | url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,11914_2264046,00.html | date=28 April 2003 | access-date=22 March 2009 | archive-date=18 October 2012 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121018135834/http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,11914_2264046,00.html | url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title=Huntelaar class shines through |publisher=[[UEFA]] |url=http://www.uefa.com/magazine/news/Kind=512/newsId=383257.html |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060115053727/http://www.uefa.com/magazine/news/Kind%3D512/newsId

In 2018, the [[United States Department of Justice|U.S. Department of Justice]] under the [[First presidency of Donald Trump|Trump administration]] seized Swalwell's personal data.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Benner|first1=Katie|last2=Fandos|first2=Nicholas|last3=Schmidt|first3=Michael S.|last4=Goldman|first4=Adam|date=June 11, 2021|title=Hunting Leaks, Trump Officials Focused on Democrats in Congress|language=en-US|work=[[The New York Times]]|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2021/06/10/us/politics/justice-department-leaks-trump-administration.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20211228/https://www.nytimes.com/2021/06/10/us/politics/justice-department-leaks-trump-administration.html |archive-date=December 28, 2021 |url-access=limited|access-date=July 2, 2021|issn=0362-4331}}{{cbignore}}</ref> The [[Trump administration data seizure|record seizure]] also targeted [[Adam Schiff]], who chaired the House Intelligence Committee.<ref>{{Cite web|date=June 11, 2021|title=Trump DOJ seized data from House Democrats in leaks probe|url=https://apnews.com/article/government-and-politics-donald-trump-5b6f6bf0daf8bccf106a90d2ff52686c|access-date=July 2, 2021|website=AP NEWS|language=en|archive-date=June 11, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210611140943/https://apnews.com/article/government-and-politics-donald-trump-5b6f6bf0daf8bccf106a90d2ff52686c|url-status=live}}</ref>
By the start of the [[118th United States Congress|118th US Congress]], Swalwell had voted in line with [[Joe Biden]]'s stated position 99.1% of the time.<ref>{{cite web  |first1=Anna |last1=Wiederkehr |first2=Aaron |last2=Bycoffe |title=Does Your Member Of Congress Vote With Or Against Biden? |url=https://projects.fivethirtyeight.com/biden-congress-votes/eric-swalwell/ |website=[[FiveThirtyEight]]|access-date=March 25, 2026 |language=en |date=January 3, 2023 |archive-date=January 17, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250117061141/https://projects.fivethirtyeight.com/biden-congress-votes/eric-swalwell/https://web.archive.org/web/20211029163339/https://projects.fivethirtyeight.com/biden-congress-votes/eric-swalwell/ |url-status=dead }}</ref>

title: Paul Lapun
comment: fix parameter(s), replaced: honorific-prefix → honorific_prefix, honorific-suffix → honorific_suffix
diff_added:
| honorific_prefix =
| honorific_suffix =
| successor4      =
Having resolved the issue of compensation, Lapun then turned his attention to the question of independence for Bougainville. In 1969 he was one of the founders of the Bougainville secessionist movement and in the same year he became patron of the pro-independence ''Napidakoe Navitu'' movement. Support for the secessionists increased as the result of the government wanting to remove people from their land to make way for [[Arawa, Bougainville|Arawa]], a new town to serve the mine, and after two civil servants from Bougainville were murdered in [[Goroka]] in the PNG highlands. Lapun thus found himself in a contradictory position of being a leading member of the Pangu Party, which stressed national unity, and being active in the movement that wanted to secede from PNG. In 1977 he failed to be re-elected as younger secessionists, who had more appeal for the electorate, had emerged with more of a confrontational approach than that being followed by Lapun.<ref name=TokPisin/><ref name=PIBA/><ref name=Ogan/><ref name=Regan/><ref name=Colonial/><ref name="Griffin">{{cite book |last1=Griffin |first1=James |last2=May |first2=R.J. |title=Napidakoe Navitu. title: Gabe Cupps
comment: /* Early life and high school career */
diff_added: Cupps grew up on a farm in [[St. Paris, Ohio]], before moving to [[Centerville, Ohio]], in the third grade.<ref name="OMB">{{cite web|url=https://www.ohsaa.org/news-media/centervilles-gabe-cupps-named-ohio-mr-basketball|title=Grateful for the Game, Cupps Named Ohio Mr. Basketball|work=The Centerville Dispatch|date=March 30, 2022|access-date=November 1, 2023}}</ref> His father Brook has been the head coach of the [[Centerville High School (Ohio)|Centerville High School]] Elks boys' basketball team since 2013.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.linkedin.com/in/brook-cupps-23864457|title=Brook Cupps |work=[[LinkedIn]]|date=November 1, 2023|access-date=November 1, 2023}}{{self-published source|date=December 2023}}</ref> Before that, he was the head coach and athletic director at [[Graham High School (St. Paris, Ohio)|Graham High School]] in St. Paris. As a result, Cupps was introduced to basketball at a young age. He absorbed his dad's work ethic according to his older sister Ally, "I watch how hard Gabe works, and then watch how hard my dad works. My whole life he’s just been basketball, basketball, basketball. And he loves his guys, and he gives them absolutely everything that he has. And then to see Gabe mirror that and to see how grounded he is..."<ref name= "Era">{{cite web|url=https://www.daytondailynews.com/sports/end-of-an-era-for-centervilles-father-son-duo/JPMBIOWIXVHY3HVFU3MQOPVKD4/|title=End of an era for Centerville's father-son duo|work=Dayton Daily News|date=March 21, 2023|access-date=November 1, 2023}}</ref> As a child, Cupps worked on his skills at home in the basement. He would pick a spot on the wall and practice passing the ball to that exact spot 50 straight times with each hand, and his dad would reward him with stickers: "I had a giant sticker board in my room, and I put all the stickers I got on it, so I could keep track. It was setting mini-goals for me, and I think that helped develop my passion."<ref name="OMB" />

title: Sam Dunell
diff_added: '''Samuel Luke Dunell''' (born 21 February 1990) is an [[Australian rules football]] player who played 12 games over 3 seasons for the [[St Kilda Football Club]] in the [[Australian Football League]] (AFL) He was recruited by the [[Williamstown Football Club|Williamstown]] in the [[Victorian Football League]] (VFL) from 2015-2019 where he played 100 games for them, kicking 158 goals. He played for Victoria against SA in 2016 and was twice named in the VFL Team of the Year. He finished 2nd in the [[J. J. Liston Trophy|VFL Liston trophy]] in 2015 capping off the season with an all conquering premiership win against [[Box Hill Football Club|Box Hill]] at [[Docklands Stadium|Docklands]]. At one stage he was considered the best player in the VFL. He was Club leading goalkicker in 2015 with 41 majors and 2016 with 37 goals, and was runner-up for Williamstown Football Club best and fairest in 2015 and in third place in 2016. He received life membership in 2019 after his final game for Williamstown. He returned to Sandringham Football Club in 2020 and won there Best n Fairest after being runnerup twice in 2013 and 2014.
diff_removed: '''Samuel Luke Dunell''' (born 21 February 1990) is an [[Australian rules football]] player who played for the [[St Kilda Football Club]] in the [[Australian Football League]] (AFL) and then played for [[Williamstown Football Club|Williamstown]] in the [[Victorian Football League]] (VFL) from 2015-2019 where he played 100 games for them, kicking 158 goals. He played for Victoria against SA in 2016 and was twice named in the VFL Team of the Year. He finished 2nd in the [[J. J. Liston Trophy|VFL Liston trophy]] in 2015 capping off the season with an all conquering premiership win against [[Box Hill Football Club|Box Hill]] at [[Docklands Stadium|Docklands]]. At one stage he was considered the best player in the VFL. He was Club leading goalkicker in 2015 with 41 majors and 2016 with 37 goals, and was runner-up in the Club best and fairest in 2015 and in third place in 2016. He received life membership in 2019 after his final game for Williamstown in the 2019 grand defeat defeat by Richmond.

| align = right

===2020 census===

As of the [[2020 United States census|2020 census]], Holtsville had a population of 18,937. The median age was 42.3 years. 19.8% of residents were under the age of 18 and 15.9% of residents were 65 years of age or older. For every 100 females there were 94.0 males, and for every 100 females age 18 and over there were 91.8 males age 18 and over.<ref name="Census2020DP">{{cite web|title=2020 Decennial Census Demographic Profile (DP1)|url=https://api.census.gov/data/2020/dec/dp?get=NAME%2CDP1_0021P%2CDP1_0024P%2CDP1_0025C%2CDP1_0049C%2CDP1_0045C%2CDP1_0069C%2CDP1_0073C%2CDP1_0125P%2CDP1_0126P%2CDP1_0129P%2CDP1_0133P%2CDP1_0137P%2CDP1_0138P%2CDP1_0139P%2CDP1_0141P%2CDP1_0142P%2CDP1_0143P%2CDP1_0145P%2CDP1_0146P%2CDP1_0147C%2CDP1_0148C%2CDP1_0149C%2CDP1_0156C%2CDP1_0157C%2CDP1_0158C%2CDP1_0159P%2CDP1_0160P&for=place%3A35254&in=state%3A36|website=United States Census Bureau|year=2021|access-date=March 26, 2026|df=mdy}}</ref><ref name="Census2020PL"/>

100.0% of residents lived in urban areas, while 0.0% lived in rural areas.<ref name="Census2020DHC">{{cite web|title=2020 Decennial Census Demographic and Housing Characteristics (DHC)|url=https://api.census.gov/data/2020/dec/dhc?get=NAME%2CP2_002N%2CP2_003N&for=place%3A35254&in=state%3A36|website=United States Census Bureau|year=2023|access-date=March 26, 2026|df=mdy}}</ref>

There were 6,448 households in Holtsville, of which 33.3% had children under the age of 18 living in them. Of all households, 58.6% were married-couple households, 13.6% were households with a male householder and no spouse or partner present, and 22.0% were households with a female householder and no spouse or partner present. About 20.1% of all households were made up of individuals and 9.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older.<ref name="Census2020DP"/>

There were 6,646 housing units, of which 3.0% were vacant. The homeowner vacancy rate was 0.7% and the rental vacancy rate was 3.4%.<ref name="Census2020DP"/>

The matador's estoc was typically a shorter one-handed thrusting sword that was around {{convert|88|cm|abbr=on}} in length.<ref>see: [[:es:Estoque de toreo|Estoque de toreo]] (Spanish)</ref>. Older forms have been shorter, some being 55cm in length.

title: Paul O'Kane
diff_added:
| honorific_suffix    = [[Member of the Scottish Parliament|MSP]]
He was placed fourth on the party list for the [[West Scotland (Scottish Parliament electoral region)|West Scotland region]], and was one of the three successful Labour candidates to be elected.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Scottish Election 2021: List MSPs confirmed for West Scotland|url=https://www.helensburghadvertiser.co.uk/news/19289234.scottish-election-2021-list-msps-confirmed-west-scotland/|access-date=2021-05-08|website=Helensburgh Advertiser|date=8 May 2021 |language=en|archive-date=2021-05-08|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210508205716/https://www.helensburghadvertiser.co.uk/news/19289234.scottish-election-2021-list-msps-confirmed-west-scotland/|url-status=live}}</ref>
O'Kane backed a Scottish Labour amendment in a Challenge Poverty Week debate in the Scottish Parliament on the UK Government’s decision to introduce means-testing for the Winter Fuel Payment, the amendment called ''for the need to support vulnerable people through the cost of living crisis and over winter with energy bills; welcomes, therefore, the announcement by the UK Government to extend the Household Support Fund, which will deliver an estimated £41 million in Barnett consequential funding, and calls on the Scottish Government to use this funding to deliver a package of support to help low-income pensioners and households by re-establishing the Fuel Insecurity Fund''.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.parliament.scot/chamber-and-committees/votes-and-motions/S6M-14820-3|title=*|website=www.parliament.scot}}</ref> The amendment in the name of Anas Sarwar fell and Paul O'Kane voted against the Scottish Government motion.
Scottish Labour proposed re-introduction of a winter fuel payment for the majority of pensioners if it were to win the 2026 Scottish election. Paul O'Kane tabled amendments to the Social Security (Amendment) (Scotland) Bill in November 2024 which would have re-instated the payment for all pensioners in Scotland by November 2025, he also proposed a taper mechanism to claw back the payment from the wealthiest pensioners.<ref>{{cite web |title=Winter Fuel Payment: Scottish Labour launches bid to force SNP to bring back universal entitlement |date=25 November 2024 |url=https://www.holyrood.com/news/view,winter-fuel-payment-scottish-labour-launches-bid-to-force-snp-to-bring-back-universal-entitlement}}</ref> the SNP voted against these amendments <ref>{{cite web | title= |url=https://www.parliament.scot/chamber-and-committees/official-report/search-what-was-said-in-parliament/meeting-of-parliament-03-12-2024?meeting=16139&iob=137847#orscontributions_M3750E441P777C2657340}}</ref>

title: Awa Fam
diff_added:
In July 2022, Fam represented [[Spain women's national under-16 and under-17 basketball team|Spain]] at the [[2022 FIBA Under-17 Women's Basketball World Cup|2022 FIBA U17 World Cup]] where she averaged 10.7 points, 9.9 rebounds and 2.6 assists per game. During the gold medal game against the [[United States women's national under-17 basketball team|United States]], she scored eight points, a team-high eight rebounds and five assists and won a silver medal.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.fiba.basketball/en/news/world-u17women-2022-news-watkins-named-tissot-mvp-to-lead-all-star-five-in-debrecen |title=Watkins named TISSOT MVP to lead All-Star Five in Debrecen |website=fiba.basketball |date=17 July 2022 |access-date=13 August 2025}}</ref> The next month, she then represented Spain at the [[2022 FIBA U16 Women's European Championship|2022 FIBA U16 European Championship]], where she averaged a double-double of 13.1 points, 10.6 rebounds and 1.7 assists per game. During the gold medal game against [[France women's national under-17 basketball team|France]], she scored eight points and a game-high 12 rebounds and won a silver medal. She was subsequently named to the all-tournament team.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.fiba.basketball/en/news/europe-u16women-2022-news-mvp-iyana-carrion-headlines-all-star-five |title=MVP Iyana Carrion headlines All-Star Five |website=fiba.basketball |date=27 August 2022 |access-date=13 August 2025}}</ref>
Fam represented [[Spain women's national under-18 and under-19 basketball team|Spain]] at the [[2023 FIBA Under-19 Women's Basketball World Cup|2023 FIBA U19 World Cup]] where she averaged 10.7 points, 8.1 rebounds and 2.1 assists per game. During the gold medal game against the [[United States women's national under-19 basketball team|United States]], she scored 11 points, five rebounds, three assists, and a game-high three blocks, and won a silver medal. She was subsequently named to the [[FIBA Under-19 Women's Basketball World Cup All-Tournament Team|FIBA U19 World Cup All-Second Team]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.fiba.basketball/world/u19women/2023/news/fiba-u19-women-s-world-cup-2023-all-second-team-best-coach-and-defensive-player-awards |title=FIBA U19 Women's World Cup 2023; All-Second Team, Best Coach and Defensive Player Awards |publisher=[[FIBA]] |date=23 July 2023 |access-date=13 August 2025}}</ref>

title: Mexican film noir
diff_added: '''Mexican film noir''' consists of the Mexican contributions to [[Film noir|film noir]], a style of [[Crime film|crime]] [[Drama (film and television)|drama]] films originating in the [[United States]] during the mid-twentieth century. Also known as ''el cine negro'' in Spanish, these movies share similarities with their [[List of film noir titles|counterparts]] in the United States, including cynical attitudes toward society, nocturnal urban settings, and tales of corruption and violence. However, ''el cine negro'' has a distinctly regional take on the sub-genre, commonly discussing then-current Mexican cultural anxieties around economic development and sexuality.
diff_removed: Mexican film noir consists of the Mexican contributions to [[Film noir|film noir]], a style of [[Crime film|crime]] [[Drama (film and television)|drama]] films originating in the [[United States]] during the mid-twentieth century. Also known as ''el cine negro'' in Spanish, these movies share similarities with their [[List of film noir titles|counterparts]] in the United States, including cynical attitudes toward society, nocturnal urban settings, and tales of corruption and violence. However, ''el cine negro'' has a distinctly regional take on the sub-genre, commonly discussing then-current Mexican cultural anxieties around economic development and sexuality.

title: Trevor McDougall
comment: added Honorary Membership of the European Geosciences Union
diff_added:
McDougall was elected a [[List of Fellows of the Royal Society elected in 2012|Fellow of the Royal Society in 2012]].  He is also a fellow of the [[Australian Academy of Science]] (1997), the [[CSIRO]] (2007), the [[Australian Meteorological and Oceanographic Society]] (2004), the [[Institute of Physics]] (UK) (2012), the [[Royal Society of New South Wales]] (2015),<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.royalsoc.org.au/about-us/fellows/|title = Fellows of the Royal Society of New South Wales|date = 16 December 2022}}</ref> the [[American Geophysical Union]] (2018),<ref>{{cite web|url=https://eos.org/agu-news/2018-class-of-agu-fellows-announced|title=2018 AGU Fellows announced|website=eos.org/agu-news/2018-class-of-agu-fellows-announced|date=9 August 2018}}</ref> and the [[International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics]] (2023). He was awarded Honorary Membership of the [[European Geosciences Union]] in 2025. His other awards include:
* Alfred Wegener Medal, 2025, awarded by the [[European Geosciences Union]], ''for outstanding contributions to ocean science that have fundamentally changed how we understand the ocean and its role in shaping the Earth’s climate''

title: Paul Pearce
comment: fix parameter(s), replaced: honorific-prefix → honorific_prefix, honorific-suffix → honorific_suffix

'''Paul Ronald Pearce''' (born 7 February 1956{{citation needed|date=November 2025}}) is an Australian former politician. He was a member of the [[New South Wales Legislative Assembly]] representing [[Electoral district of Coogee|Coogee]] between 2003 and [[2011 New South Wales state election|2011]] for the [[Australian Labor Party (New South Wales Branch)|Labor Party]] and Mayor of Waverley from 1997 to 2004.
Pearce was educated at Granville Public School, Bronte Public School (where he was Dux) and [[Sydney Boys High School]].{{citation needed|date=November 2025}} He won academic prizes for 1st Level Ancient History and 1st Level Economics. He has received a [[Bachelor of Arts]] and [[Master of Arts]] from the [[University of Sydney]] and a [[Bachelor of Laws]] (Hons) from the [[University of London External Programme]]. He obtained an International Practice Diploma in International Human Rights Law from the [[International Bar Association]]. He has received a Master of Laws from [[Aberystwyth University]] in International Human Rights and Humanitarian Law. His thesis was entitled "Anti-terrorism laws - time for a rethink?".<ref>{{Cite NSW Parliament |name=Mr Paul Ronald Pearce, MA, LLB (1956- ) |former=Yes |access-date=30 April 2019 |id=2168}}</ref>
He joined the Australian Labor Party in 1974. He was elected to the executive of NSW Young Labor in 1978, became National Secretary of Australian Young Labor in 1980 and President of Australian Young Labor in 1981. He was also Senior Vice President of NSW Young Labor in 1981. In 1976, he was employed as a legal search clerk and joined the Federated Clerks Union (now the USU). He subsequently was self-employed in the textile industry between 1978 and 1997. He contested Waverley Ward of Waverley Council in 1980 but was not elected. He recontested in 1983 and was elected. He was a councillor of [[Waverley Municipal Council]] between 1983 and 2004, serving as Mayor of Waverley between 1997 and 2004. From 1996 to 2001, he was the director of the Southern Sydney Waste Management Board.<ref>{{cite web|title       = Council Members|publisher = [[University of New South Wales]]|url = http://www.secretariat.unsw.edu.au/council/Council_member_biographies.htm|accessdate = 2007-02-15|url-status = dead|archiveurl = https://web.archive.org/web/20060918060419/http://www.secretariat.unsw.edu.au/council/Council_member_biographies.htm|archivedate = 18 September 2006}}</ref> He stood unsuccessfully for the federal seat of [[Division of Wentworth|Wentworth]] in 1993, 1996 and 1998.<ref>{{cite web| title =Index of candidates for the House of Representatives and the Senate 1901–2002| publisher =Adam Carr| url =http://psephos.adam-carr.net/countries/a/australia/candidates/p.txt| accessdate = 2007-02-15 }}</ref> In 1998, he took the historically safe Liberal seat of Wentworth to preferences for the first time in over 50 years.{{citation needed|date=November 2025}}

title: Charles Herbert Harington
comment: deleted link to crown colony. he died before that existed
diff_added:
'''Charles Herbert Harington''' was an important figure in the history of [[North Borneo]] (present day [[Sabah]]). He was the longest-serving [[Commandant]] of the [[British North Borneo Constabulary]] in its history, and also occupied other duties within the colony's government. He led many [[punitive expedition]]s into the interior of the colony, but is most–known as leading the expedition that killed [[Mat Salleh (Person)|Mat Salleh]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Rutter |first=Owen |url=http://archive.org/details/cu31924023151933 |title=British North Borneo : An Account of its History, Resources, and Native Tribes |date=1922 |publisher=London : Constable & Co. Ltd. |others=Cornell University Library |pages=149, 196, 204–209, 213}}</ref>
In 1896, some time after the Jameson Raid, Harington joined the service of the [[North Borneo Chartered Company]].<ref name=":14">{{Cite web |date=1 June 1926 |title=Mail Edition |url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/straitsechomail19260601-1.2.78 |publisher=The Straits Echo |page=568}}</ref> The company, as it was often called, was the sole governing authority over [[British North Borneo]], what was known as a [[British protectorate]], but not a colony. After an interview at the company's London Headquarters on [[Leadenhall Street]], where all new recruits to the Constabulary were interviewed, he was sent to North Borneo.<ref name=":1" />
diff_removed:
'''Charles Herbert Harington''' was an important figure in the history of [[North Borneo]], later the [[Crown Colony of North Borneo]]. He was the longest-serving [[Commandant]] of the [[British North Borneo Constabulary]] in its history, and also occupied other duties within the colony's government. He led many [[punitive expedition]]s into the interior of the colony, but is most–known as leading the expedition that killed [[Mat Salleh (Person)|Mat Salleh]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Rutter |first=Owen |url=http://archive.org/details/cu31924023151933 |title=British North Borneo : An Account of its History, Resources, and Native Tribes |date=1922 |publisher=London : Constable & Co. Ltd. |others=Cornell University Library |pages=149, 196, 204–209, 213}}</ref>
In 1896, some time after the Jameson Raid, Harington joined the service of the [[North Borneo Chartered Company]].<ref name=":14">{{Cite web |date=1 June 1926 |title=Mail Edition |url=https://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/digitised/article/straitsechomail19260601-1.2.78 |publisher=The Straits Echo |page=568}}</ref> The company, as it was often called, was the sole governing authority over [[British North Borneo]] (today called [[Sabah]]), what was known as a [[British protectorate]], but not a colony. After an interview at the company's London Headquarters on [[Leadenhall Street]], where all new recruits to the Constabulary were interviewed, he was sent to North Borneo.<ref name=":1" />

title: The Captive (2014 film)
diff_added: In 2026, ''The Captive'' achieved significant viewership on Netflix, becoming the streaming service's ninth most popular film by the week of 18-24 March.<ref>{{cite web |title=TOP 10 on Netflix in the United States on March 18, 2026 |url=https://flixpatrol.com/top10/netflix/united-states/2026-03-18/ |website=FlixPatrol.com |access-date=21 March 2026}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=James |first1=Faye |title=The little-known Ryan Reynolds 2014 crime thriller that unexpectedly topped film charts 12 years later |journal=[[Hello Magazine]] |date=24 March 2026 |url=https://www.hellomagazine.com/us/891396/the-little-known-ryan-reynolds-2014-crime-thriller-that-unexpectedly-topped-film-charts-12-years-later/ |access-date=26 March 2026}}</ref> Jeremy Smith attributes the sudden interest in the film to contemporary focus on [[Jeffrey Epstein]]'s child trafficking ring.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Smith |first1=Jeremy |title=Ryan Reynolds And Rosario Dawson's Forgotten 2014 Thriller Blows Up On Netflix |url=https://www.yahoo.com/entertainment/movies/articles/ryan-reynolds-rosario-dawsons-forgotten-220000554.html |website=[[Yahoo Entertainment]] |access-date=21 March 2026}}</ref>
diff_removed: In 2026, ''The Captive'' achieved significant viewership on Netflix, becoming the streaming service's ninth most popular film by 18 March.<ref>{{cite web |title=TOP 10 on Netflix in the United States on March 18, 2026 |url=https://flixpatrol.com/top10/netflix/united-states/2026-03-18/ |website=FlixPatrol.com |access-date=21 March 2026}}</ref> Jeremy Smith attributes the sudden interest in the film to contemporary focus on [[Jeffrey Epstein]]'s child trafficking ring.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Smith |first1=Jeremy |title=Ryan Reynolds And Rosario Dawson's Forgotten 2014 Thriller Blows Up On Netflix |url=https://www.yahoo.com/entertainment/movies/articles/ryan-reynolds-rosario-dawsons-forgotten-220000554.html |website=[[Yahoo Entertainment]] |access-date=21 March 2026}}</ref>
